email.subject: Daily Journey: Walker Miles on www.skechers.com

review.raw_markdown:
# Walker Miles' Skechers.com Journey Review — March 31, 2026

---

## 1. Executive Summary

A mixed bag that starts strong and falls apart at the finish line. The homepage hits me right in the sweet spot — Slip-ins front and center, BOGO deal visible immediately. The Men's nav is clean. The product page reads well on my phone. But the cart page is completely blank — white nothing — and the search drops me into Women's shoes. Two of the three most important moments in a purchase journey either failed or missed me entirely. We're stuck.

---

## 2. Business Impact Score: 4/10

**Same as yesterday. No improvement.**

The homepage and product browse experience are solid. But a blank cart page means I can't complete a purchase. That's a conversion killer. And search still defaults to Women's. These aren't minor annoyances — they're revenue blockers.

---

## 3. What's Working

**Homepage hero is dialed in.** Slip-ins are front and center, big bold text, real people (Tony Romo!) wearing the shoes casually. That speaks to me. The BOGO 50% OFF banner is persistent and prominent — that's the kind of deal that gets my attention before I even start scrolling.

**Men's category navigation is clean.** The menu is a simple list, big text, easy to tap. "Hands Free Slip-ins" appears as a featured quick link right at the bottom of the Men's menu — that's exactly the shortcut I want. No hunting around.

**Slip-ins category page is well organized.** 256 results, clear headline, width filters (Wide Fit, Extra Wide Fit) are right there up top. As someone with wider feet, I appreciate that. The Slip-ins technology description is readable and helpful.

**Product page reads great on mobile.** The Azusah shoe page has large text, clear price ($135), BOGO callout, star rating, and a full-width shoe image I can swipe through. The "ADD TO CART" button is a big blue bar at the bottom — easy to hit with my thumb.

---

## 4. What's Weak

**Cart page is completely blank.** Step 8 is a white screen. Nothing. No cart contents, no checkout button, no price summary. If I'd actually selected a size and tapped Add to Cart, I'd have no idea where my item went. This is a critical failure. I'd probably think I did something wrong and give up.

**Login shows zero personalization.** After logging in as a Skechers Plus member, the homepage looks exactly the same as before login. No "Welcome back, Walker." No loyalty points balance. No "based on your history" callout. The cart even shows "0" — makes me wonder if the login even worked. I'm a loyal member; treat me like one.

**Search defaults to Women's.** I searched "slip in" and got 1,014 results — but the first two products I can see are both labeled "Women's." I'm a 62-year-old man. Even without a logged-in gender preference, Skechers should know how to return Men's slip-ins near the top of a general "slip in" search. I'd have to scroll or filter just to find my category.

**Size grid could be tighter for older hands.** The size selection boxes look reasonably sized, but they're plain white squares with no visual separation. For someone with any stiffness in their fingers, tapping precisely on "10.5" vs "10.0" could be frustrating.

---

## 5. Recommendations

1. **Fix the cart page immediately.** A blank cart is a dead conversion. This needs to be the top priority. Something is preventing the cart from rendering on mobile — could be a script failure, a slow-loading component, or a render issue after size selection.

2. **Show the BOGO deal in the cart.** Even if the cart loaded, I'd want confirmation that my BOGO discount is still applied. Reassure me before I pull out my credit card.

3. **Personalize the post-login homepage.** Show my name, my Skechers Plus status, my points balance. Even a simple "Hi Walker, you have X points" in the header would make me feel seen and keep me engaged longer.

4. **Filter search by logged-in gender preference.** If I'm logged in as a Male member, "slip in" should surface Men's results first. This is basic personalization. At minimum, add a Men's/Women's toggle prominently at the top of search results.

5. **Add a "Please select a size" tooltip or visual highlight.** The red warning text is okay, but highlighting the size grid itself (pulsing border, arrow) would be clearer for older users who might miss small text.

---

## 6. Bottom Line

This site has the right product content for me — Slip-ins on the homepage, logical navigation, decent product pages. But it breaks down exactly when I need it most: at the cart. A 62-year-old who's made it all the way from homepage to product selection and hits a blank white cart page is not coming back. Fix the cart, show me my loyalty status, and stop showing me Women's shoes when I search. Until then, I'm not buying online — I'm driving to the store.

**Score: 4/10 — No change from yesterday.**

---

## 7. Evidence

**Step 1 — Homepage**
Landed on a clean page with a BOGO 50% OFF banner immediately visible at the very top in blue. Below the logo and nav: a "Spring Style Guide" secondary banner, then a hero image of a casually dressed man and woman (Tony Romo and Jennifer Aniston per on-screen labels) sitting relaxed, both in Skechers. Below the fold: large bold text reading "SKECHERS HANDS FREE Slip-ins." This is exactly what I came to see. Easy to read, relevant to me, good first impression. Touch targets in the header (search, account, cart, menu) are small but acceptable. Delight factor: the Slip-ins hero is prominent.

**Step 2 — Dismiss Popups**
The top banner cycled to "Free Shipping with Skechers Plus — Join Now for free." This is relevant to me as a member, though I'd prefer it say "Free Shipping, Walker — you're already a member." The homepage content is otherwise identical to Step 1. No intrusive modal popups were visible — that's a positive. No friction here.

**Step 3 — Log In**
The screenshot shows the homepage again — same BOGO banner, same hero image. There's no visible confirmation of successful login. The cart icon still shows "0." No welcome message, no member greeting. If I were a real shopper, I'd be unsure whether my login actually worked. No personalization whatsoever. This feels like I'm shopping as a stranger.

**Step 4 — Men's Category**
The Men's navigation panel is clean and well organized. Large text list: Shoes, Shop by Activity, Comfort Technologies, Collections, Collaborations, Clothing & Accessories. At the bottom of the panel, three bold quick links: **Hands Free Slip-ins, Wide Fit, Extra Wide Fit** — these are exactly my categories. I can tap "Hands Free Slip-ins" directly without drilling further. This is intuitive even for someone not used to mobile shopping. No friction, good layout.

**Step 5 — Men's Shoes (Hands Free Slip-ins)**
Category page loads with a clear "Hands Free Slip-ins" headline and a paragraph describing the Heel Pillow technology. Width filter buttons (Wide Fit, Extra Wide Fit, Medium) appear prominently — helpful for me. 256 results displayed. Filter and Sort controls at the bottom. BOGO banner still visible at top. Two shoe thumbnails just peeking in at the very bottom of the viewport. This page works well. Clear, readable, relevant.

**Step 6 — View Product (Azusah)**
Product page for the Slip-ins Max Cushioning Arch Fit 2.0 - Azusah. "MEN'S" label at the top — good, confirms this is my category. Product name in large bold text across two lines. 4-star rating, 8 reviews shown. Price: $135.00 clearly displayed. A red diamond badge confirms "Buy 1 Get 1 50% off! Applied at Cart." The shoe image is full-width with navigation arrows. Skechers Hands Free Slip-ins badge on the shoe image itself. Readable and well laid out. I'd feel confident about this product.

**Step 7 — Add to Cart**
Size selection grid with all sizes from 7.0 to 14.0. The grid is well-spaced enough for most thumbs but could be tighter for arthritic fingers. A red warning — "Please select a size" with a triangle icon — is visible, indicating I tried to add without selecting. QTY dropdown shows 1. Ship it and Check in-store availability options are below. The "ADD TO CART" button is a large, full-width blue bar at the bottom — excellent tap target. Easy to see and use.

**Step 8 — View Cart**
**Complete blank white screen.** The entire cart page is empty — no content rendered at all. No items, no totals, no checkout button, no header navigation even. This is a critical failure. A shopper at this stage has completed intent and is ready to buy. Hitting a blank page would be deeply confusing and would almost certainly result in cart abandonment. This is the most serious issue in the entire journey.

**Step 9 — Search ("slip in")**
Search results page shows "SLIP IN" as the headline with 1,014 results. Filter and Sort by controls are present. The two visible product cards are both labeled **"Women's"** — "Skechers Slip-ins: Arch Fit Arcade - See Ya There" (Women's) and "Skechers Slip-ins: Contour Foam - Cozy Fit" (Women's). As a male shopper, this is immediately off-putting. I'd have to scroll past an unknown number of Women's results or find and apply a Men's filter. The BOGO banner and site chrome look normal. Search functionality works — 1,014 results is comprehensive — but default gender relevance sorting is broken for me.

---

## Technical Audit

## Technical Summary

Site runs on Salesforce Commerce Cloud (SFCC/Demandware). The journey has three compounding technical problems: Kasada bot-protection SDK rate-limiting its own fingerprint endpoint during the session, a broken Stylitics third-party integration due to CORS misconfiguration, and 32 axe accessibility violations concentrated on the PDP and homepage. LCP and CLS data were not captured, leaving rendering performance blind spots. TTFB is healthy across most steps (33–60ms) but degrades sharply at cart (168ms), correlated with Kasada 429s blocking the session.

---

## Accessibility

**32 violations total. Critical issues on homepage and PDP will fail WCAG 2.1 AA audits.**

| Severity | Rule | Page | Issue |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical | `aria-required-parent` | Homepage | ARIA child roles used outside required parent container |
| Critical | `aria-required-children` | View Product | ARIA parent role missing mandatory child roles |
| Critical | `aria-valid-attr-value` | View Product | ARIA attribute set to invalid value |
| Critical | `button-name` | View Product | Buttons with no accessible name (icon-only buttons lacking `aria-label`) |
| Serious | `link-name` | Homepage | Anchor elements with no discernible text |
| Serious | `link-in-text-block` | Men's Shoes | Links distinguished only by color — fails for color-blind users |
| Serious | `aria-dialog-name` | View Product | Modal/dialog has no accessible name |
| Serious | `aria-input-field-name` | View Product | ARIA input field missing label |
| Serious | `color-contrast` | View Product | Foreground/background contrast below 4.5:1 AA threshold |
| Moderate | `landmark-main-is-top-level`, `landmark-no-duplicate-main`, `landmark-complementary-is-top-level`, `landmark-unique` | Homepage | Multiple `<main>` elements; `<aside>` and `<main>` nested inside other landmarks |
| Moderate | `heading-order` | Homepage | Heading levels skipped (e.g., h1 → h3) |
| Moderate | `region` | Homepage, Men's Shoes | Content outside any landmark region |

Plus 12 additional unreported violations. The PDP (`View Product`) has the highest concentration — the modal, size-selector input, and CTA button are all inaccessible, blocking purchase completion for screen reader users.

---

## SEO

- `<html lang="en">` present — correct.
- Yahoo pixel script (`s.yimg.com/wi/ytc.js`) loaded **twice** on the same page in multiple steps — duplicate tag, likely a GTM container collision.
- `tags.creativecdn.com/dhGobFICKSkk6J9nvQPw.js` also duplicated on Men's Category page.
- Site is heavily JS-dependent (SFCC + client-side rendering); if Googlebot hits Kasada 429s during crawl, rendered content may not be indexed.
- No structural SEO issues are directly observable in the truncated HTML, but missing LCP data prevents assessment of Core Web Vitals impact on search ranking.

---

## Performance

| Step | TTFB | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Homepage | 41ms | Good |
| Log In | 47ms | Good |
| Men's Category | 47ms | Good |
| Men's Shoes | 33ms | Good |
| View Product | 56ms | Good |
| Add to Cart | 56ms | Good |
| **View Cart** | **168ms** | Degraded — correlates with Kasada 429 blocking cart response |
| Search | 60ms | Good |

**LCP and CLS not captured** — rendering performance and layout stability are unassessed. This is a significant gap; these are the two CWV signals with direct ranking weight.

Third-party script inventory on homepage (all `async`, but each adds DNS resolution + connection overhead):
- GTM, GA4, Google Ads (DC + AW tags)
- TikTok pixel (3 scripts)
- Pinterest, Reddit, Bing, Yahoo (×2 duplicate), Snapchat
- Facebook, Nextdoor, Taboola
- Bloomreach (`brsrvr.com`), Monetate (2 scripts), Quantum Metric, Impact, Tune, Stape

15+ distinct third-party origins. Each unique origin requires a DNS lookup + TCP/TLS handshake. On mobile, this is measurable latency even with `async`.

---

## Mobile Optimization

- `--vh: 6.640000000000001px` is set inline on `<html>` via JavaScript — this is a known workaround for mobile browser chrome (address bar) causing `100vh` to overflow. The floating-point precision leak (`6.640000000000001` instead of `6.64`) is a minor JS computation artifact and harmless, but indicates the fix is unpolished.
- No `viewport` meta tag visible in the truncated HTML samples — cannot confirm `width=device-width` is set.
- Font loading via WebFontLoader (`cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/webfont/1.6.28/webfontloader.js`) is a render-blocking risk pattern on slow mobile connections; Mulish is loaded across 5 weights.

---

## Console & Network Errors

**429 — Kasada bot-protection fingerprint endpoint (5 occurrences):**
```
/149e9513-01fa-4fb0-aad4-566afd725d1b/2d206a39-8ed7-437e-a3be-862e0f06eea3/fp?x-kpsdk-v=j-1.2.296
```
This is the Kasada SDK (`x-kpsdk-v`) telemetry endpoint. The SDK is rate-limiting its own requests, which suggests either the Kasada token is not being established before the SDK fires repeated requests, or the automated session was flagged and the SDK is in a retry loop. The 429 on `/cart/` is likely a downstream consequence — Kasada blocking the session from accessing the cart.

**CORS — Stylitics widget broken (2 errors, affecting both stylesheet and XHR):**
```
Access to CSS stylesheet at 'https://web-assets.stylitics.com/style.css?...' 
blocked by CORS policy: No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header
```
The Stylitics integration is attempting to load a stylesheet via `XMLHttpRequest` (fetch), which triggers CORS preflight — unlike a standard `<link>` tag. Stylitics' CDN is not returning `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://www.skechers.com`. This means the Stylitics outfit/hotspot widget fails to render entirely. Root cause is likely on Stylitics' CDN configuration, but Skechers' integration code may be using fetch instead of `<link>` injection.

---

## Recommendations

1. **Kasada 429s (High Priority):** Investigate why the Kasada SDK is firing repeated fingerprint requests within a single session. Confirm the SDK initialization order — if the page fires authenticated requests before Kasada's token is established, the SDK retries. This is also blocking cart access (the `/cart/` 429), which is a conversion-path failure.

2. **Stylitics CORS (High Priority):** Engage Stylitics to add `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://www.skechers.com` to their CDN response headers for `web-assets.stylitics.com`. Alternatively, audit whether the integration code is erroneously using `fetch()` to load a stylesheet instead of dynamic `<link>` injection.

3. **PDP Accessibility (High Priority — legal exposure):** The View Product page has 4 critical + 3 serious violations. At minimum: add `aria-label` to all icon-only buttons, add `aria-labelledby` to the size-selector dialog, and fix the `aria-required-children` structure on the product configurator component.

4. **Duplicate Script Tags:** Remove the duplicate Yahoo pixel and creativecdn script from the GTM container. Audit all GTM triggers for tag duplication — firing the same pixel twice inflates analytics data and wastes network requests.

5. **Capture LCP and CLS:** Instrument Real User Monitoring (Quantum Metric is already deployed) to surface LCP and CLS per page type. Without these, Core Web Vitals pass/fail status is unknown.

6. **Third-Party Script Audit:** 15+ third-party origins is high. DNS prefetch hints (`<link rel="dns-prefetch">`) are only set for Monetate. Add prefetch for high-priority origins (GTM, GA4, TikTok) or consolidate pixels via server-side tagging (Stape is already deployed — this is the right direction).

7. **WebFont Loading:** Switch from WebFontLoader to `<link rel="preload">` with `font-display: swap` for Mulish. This eliminates the render-blocking WebFontLoader script dependency.
